name: Two Pointers
concept:
## The idea

You already know arrays. **Two pointers** just means keeping *two* indices into the same array and moving them intelligently, instead of using two nested loops.

Two common shapes:

1. **Opposite ends** — `left` starts at `0`, `right` at `len - 1`, and they walk toward each other. Great when the array is **sorted** or when you're pairing/bounding from both sides.
2. **Same direction (fast/slow)** — both start on the left; one races ahead. (You'll see this more in Sliding Window and Linked Lists.)

The magic: each pointer only moves forward, so the whole array is processed in **one pass → O(n)** instead of O(n²).

## Python you'll pick up here

- Multiple assignment: `left, right = 0, len(nums) - 1`
- `while left < right:` loops (no manual index bookkeeping like other languages)
- Negative indexing: `nums[-1]` is the last element
- `s[i].isalnum()`, `.lower()` for string problems

Don't study Python separately — you'll absorb it from the solutions below.
whenToUse: The array is sorted (or you can sort it first)
You're looking for a pair / triplet that meets a condition
You're bounding a region from both ends (e.g. max area, palindrome check)
You want to turn an O(n²) nested loop into a single O(n) pass

problems.title: Valid Palindrome
problems.keyIdeas: Put one pointer at each end and walk inward, **skipping non-alphanumeric characters**, comparing lowercased characters. If any pair differs, it's not a palindrome. This is the gentlest possible two-pointer warm-up — the whole pattern in ~8 lines.
hints: You don't need to build a reversed string. Can you compare the ends directly?
Use `left` and `right` pointers. When `s[left]` isn't a letter/digit, move `left` forward without comparing.
Compare `s[left].lower()` with `s[right].lower()`. If they ever differ, return False; otherwise step both inward.

solutions.label: Clean two-pointer
solutions.keyIdea: Skip junk characters on each side, compare lowercased ends, converge.

solutions.label: Pythonic (filter first)
solutions.keyIdea: Build the cleaned list of lowercased alphanumerics, then check it reads the same forwards and backwards. Less optimal on space but very readable — good for learning Python slicing.

problems.description: A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters into lowercase letters and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Alphanumeric characters include letters and numbers.

problems.title: Two Sum II — Input Array Is Sorted
problems.keyIdeas: Because the array is **sorted**, put pointers at both ends. If the sum is too big, the only way to shrink it is `right -= 1`; too small → `left += 1`. Each step provably eliminates one number, so it's O(n). This 'move the side that fixes the sum' logic is the template for 3Sum next.
hints: The array is sorted — that's a huge hint. What does that let two pointers at the ends tell you about the current sum?
If numbers[left] + numbers[right] is greater than target, which pointer should move, and why?
Too big → decrease right. Too small → increase left. Equal → you found it (return 1-indexed positions).

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Try every pair of indices. Works but costs O(n²) — motivates why sorting + two pointers is powerful.

solutions.label: Optimal — two pointers from the ends
solutions.keyIdea: Sorted order means a too-large sum can only be fixed by moving the right pointer left, and vice-versa. Reach for this when the array is sorted; use a hashmap instead for unsorted arrays.

solutions.label: Alternative — hashmap
solutions.keyIdea: Store each number's index in a dict as you scan, check if the complement exists. Same O(n) time as two-pointers but uses O(n) space. Prefer this when the array is unsorted and you can't sort it; otherwise the two-pointer approach is cleaner.

problems.description: Given a 1-indexed array of integers numbers that is already sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific target number. Let these two numbers be numbers[index1] and numbers[index2] where 1 <= index1 < index2 <= numbers.length.

problems.title: Container With Most Water
problems.keyIdeas: Area = `width × min(height[left], height[right])`. Start as wide as possible (both ends). Width only shrinks as you move in, so the **only** way to possibly gain area is to move the pointer at the *shorter* wall — moving the taller one can never help. One pass, O(n).
hints: Brute force checks every pair (i, j). What's the area between two lines in terms of their heights and distance apart?
Start with the widest container: one pointer at each end. Width will only decrease from here.
Since width shrinks each step, only increasing the limiting (shorter) height can help — so always move the pointer at the shorter line.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: Try every pair of lines directly from the definition of area.

solutions.label: Optimal — two pointers (Sumanth's solution)
solutions.keyIdea: Width is maximal at the ends; area is capped by the shorter wall, so greedily move the shorter pointer inward.

problems.description: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]).

problems.title: 3Sum
problems.keyIdeas: Sort, then **fix one number** and run the classic two-pointer 'two sum on a sorted array' on the rest to find pairs that complete the triplet to zero. Sorting also makes it easy to skip duplicates. This is Two Sum II wrapped in one outer loop — O(n²).
hints: Sorting first unlocks the two-pointer trick and makes duplicate-skipping easy.
Fix index i, then find pairs in the remaining array that sum to -nums[i]. That inner search is exactly Two Sum II.
Sumanth's version factors the inner two-pointer scan into a helper and collects triplets in a set to dedupe.

solutions.label: Optimal — sort + two pointers (Sumanth's solution)
solutions.keyIdea: Fix each element; two-pointer the rest for the complementary pair. A set dedupes triplets. This is the standard approach — reach for it when you need unique triplets and can afford O(n log n) sort. The hashset alternative below avoids sorting but uses more space.
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the output set (excluding sort)

solutions.label: Optimal — skip duplicates inline (LeetCode-style)
solutions.keyIdea: Same sort + two-pointer idea, but skip duplicate values as you go so you never emit the same triplet twice — no set needed. Slightly trickier to implement correctly but saves the set space.

solutions.label: Alternative — hashset (no sort)
solutions.keyIdea: For each pair (i, j), check if -(nums[i] + nums[j]) exists in a set. Use a set of tuples to dedupe. Reach for this when you cannot sort the input or when n is small enough that O(n²) space is acceptable; otherwise the sorted two-pointer approach is cleaner and uses O(1) extra space.

problems.description: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0.

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: TODO: Add key ideas

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: TODO: Add key ideas
solutions.code:
nums = [-4,-3,-2,-1,0,0,1,2,3,4]

target = 0

validTuples = set()
nums.sort()
n = len(nums)
for i in range(0,n):
    for j in range(i+1, n):
        twoSumTarget = target - nums[i] - nums[j]
        
        l=n-1
        k=j+1
        while(k<l):
            print(i,j,k,l)
            if(twoSumTarget==nums[k] + nums[l]):
                print(i,j,k,l,"came")
                validTuples.add((nums[i],nums[j],nums[k],nums[l]))
                k += 1
                l -= 1
            elif(twoSumTarget>nums[k] + nums[l]):
                k += 1
            else:
                l -= 1
                
print(validTuples)

problems.title: count Subarrays With Fixed Bounds
problems.keyIdeas: #there are (iBad+1 to min(iMin,iMax) starting points to subarray ending at j)

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: #there are (iBad+1 to min(iMin,iMax) starting points to subarray ending at j)
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def countSubarrays(self, nums, minK: int, maxK: int) -> int:
        iMin = iMax = iBad = -1
        res = 0
        for j, val in enumerate(nums):
            if not minK<=val<=maxK: iBad = j
            if val==minK: iMin = j
            if val==maxK: iMax = j
            #there are (iBad+1 to min(iMin,iMax) starting points to subarray ending at j)
            res += max(0, min(iMin, iMax) - iBad)
        return res

problems.description: You are given an integer array nums and two integers minK and maxK.

problems.title: longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

solutions.label: Solution from repo
solutions.keyIdea: TODO: Add key ideas
solutions.code:
def lengthOfLongestSubstring(s: str) -> int:
    
    charSet = set()
    i,j  = 0,0
    n = len(s)
    if n==0: return 0
    maxLen = 1
    while(j<n):
        if s[j] in charSet:
            while s[i]!=s[j]:
                charSet.remove(s[i])
                i += 1
            i+= 1

        charSet.add(s[j])
        maxLen = max(maxLen,j-i+1)
        j += 1

    return maxLen
s = "abcabcbb"
print(lengthOfLongestSubstring(s))

problems.title: Trapping Rain Water
problems.keyIdeas: Water trapped at each index = `min(max_left, max_right) - height[i]`. The two-pointer insight: you only need to know which side has the *smaller* max — move that pointer inward and update. That lets you compute trapped water in one pass with O(1) space, instead of precomputing left/right max arrays.
hints: At any index, the water level is capped by the shorter of the tallest bar on its left and the tallest on its right.
Brute force: for each index, scan left and right to find those max heights. Can you precompute them in two O(n) passes instead?

Optimal: use two pointers starting at both ends. Track `left_max` and `right_max`. If `left_max < right_max`, you know the water at `left` is determined by `left_max`, so you can add it and move `left` forward. Mirror logic for the right side.

solutions.label: Brute force
solutions.keyIdea: For each position, scan left and right to find the max heights, then compute trapped water as min(left_max, right_max) - height[i].

solutions.label: Better — precompute max arrays
solutions.keyIdea: Precompute the max height to the left and right of each index in two O(n) passes, then compute trapped water in a third pass.

solutions.label: Optimal — two pointers O(1) space
solutions.keyIdea: Track left_max and right_max as you go. The pointer on the side with the smaller max determines water there — process it and move inward. No arrays needed. Reach for this when you need O(1) space; use the DP-arrays approach above for clearer code at the cost of O(n) space, or the stack approach below when you're already thinking in terms of monotonic decreasing sequences.

solutions.label: Alternative — monotonic stack
solutions.keyIdea: Maintain a stack of indices where heights are decreasing. When you hit a taller bar, pop indices and calculate trapped water layer-by-layer between the popped bar and the current bar, bounded by what's left on the stack. Reach for this when you're solving similar problems (largest rectangle in histogram) or when you want to compute water in horizontal slices rather than vertical columns.

problems.description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
